sentences.text: Yeah.
Oh, I didn't know that.
He was a Palestinian soldier who apparently was asking everybody, you know, did you see somebody by the name of Frumkin?
Did you see a, a man and a son?
Did you see a mother, whatever?
Everybody was asking about everybody those days.
And it just so happened I was in that camp.
And there were so few kids that to find a kid was much easier than to find an adult.
So I naturally got on the truck, and we went to Italy.
And in Italy, I lived for a couple of weeks on the army base, which was a lot of fun.
And then they put us on small sort of a kibbutz type of a thing, where there was a group of people preparatory to going to Palestine with Aliyah Bet because you couldn't enter Palestine.
The British wouldn't let you.
So there was a lot of illegal immigration.
Would you explain what Aliyah Bet?
Yeah, sure.
The British Navy, after having won the war, decided that they had nothing better to do than to not permit any Jews from Europe who survived to go to Palestine.
Palestine, at the time, was a British colony, and it had a small Jewish community, 600,000-- not very rich.
And nobody in the rest of the world would let the Jews in except the Soviets, who wanted to let them in so they could do-- get slave laborers.
America, if you didn't have a relative, you couldn't get in.
You had to wait on a quota, 10, 15, 20 years depending where you came from.
South America, you could buy a visa if you had the dollars.
Nobody did.
And Canada, New Zealand, Australia were willing to take European immigrants, but only not Jews.
Palestine said, we'll take them all.
There is no need for Jews to be refugees, to be sitting on foreign soil which is soaked in Jewish blood.
And the British said, absolutely not.
So after there was no legal immigration, they had to resort to illegal immigration called Aliyah Bet.
And they-- the Palestinians, the Israelis, tried to find whatever boats they could, whatever ships they could, and smuggled people into Palestine.
Most of them were caught by the British Navy, which was a very good Navy and very efficient.
Most of them were taken to the-- to Cypress, which was also a British colony, and put behind barbed wire with British soldiers at the gates.
And when I got to Italy, the people there were being trained for Aliyah Bet, and also trained how to grow stuff and all that.
And, and a very strange thing happened at the time, which took me a long, long time to understand.
We were in this little agricultural settlement outside of the town of Magenta in Italy.
Everybody was very kind.
Everybody was nice.
There were mostly adults, about 70 or 80 adults.
And there were about 10 or 12 kids my age, survivors from different camps.
And we got along very nicely.
Then at one point the word came through that all the kids have to be transferred to another place, which is going to be a youth camp, a [NON-ENGLISH].
And so we went.
And we got to this terrible place somewhere in the Italian Alps where it was raining all the time, and it was cold and miserable.
And there was just us and maybe 10 other kids from somewhere else.
And there was no adults.
There was a drunken Italian cook.
And we just didn't like it at all.
Then after about two weeks, we decided, the hell with it.
And we left the camp and hitchhiked our way across Italy, and got back to Magenta, where it had been so nice.
And we figured everybody will be happy to see us.
And we got back to Magenta, and surprise, surprise.
They hated us.
They didn't want to talk to us.
They were angry that we came back.
They said, why don't you go back where you came from?
Why don't you go back to the camp where you're supposed to be?
We couldn't understand it.
It broke our little children's hearts because we really liked these people and we really got along with them.
And eventually, you know, it was arranged-- we were there for about five, six days, and then the soldiers came back, the Palestinian soldiers.
And they said, you're right, this was a lousy place.
We've got a better place.
They took us to another youth camp which was nicer and many more kids, and everything was fine.
And for years I never forgave these people for being so nasty to us.
About 10 years ago, I'm walking along the street in Hollywood, maybe 15 streets ago-- 15 years ago.
And they got a sale of books outside one of the bookstores, like they have big tables-- $0.95, whatever they are.
So I pick up a book and it's called For $0.95, I'll buy the book.

I take it home, and it's written by a guy who was very much involved in illegal arms smuggling to the Haganah, to the Jewish Resistance, to the Jewish underground army under the British, because the Jewish soldiers, naturally, were stealing whatever weapons they could get and sending them illegally to Palestine.
And all kinds of very brave and very sharp things were being done.
You know, you shipped guns like tractors, and pianos were really machine guns, and God knows what else.
Well, I read the book, and it's very fascinating.
And I come across a chapter entitled 'Magenta,' which is the name of the place we were at.
And it says the main center of arms packing and trans shipping activity in Italy was a little agricultural settlement called Magenta.
Naturally, they didn't want any kids around.
They had nothing against us.
They just didn't want any kids hanging around while they were doing this kind of stuff where everybody could get busted by the British any minute.
And then I forgave them many years later.
I felt much better about it.
Anyway, I stayed on in Italy.
My mother, who was also asking everybody she met, you know, have you seen my kid?
Have you seen my husband?
Where was she?
She was in Poland.
She was liberated by the Russians.
She was left for dead with typhus, and when the Russians came they found all those bodies lying around.
And some of them were still alive, so they put her-- and her sister, as a matter of fact, was in the same camp.
Her sister survived because her little girl was kept by Gentiles in Lithuania.
If she had had a little girl, it would also be bad.
But she survived.
And then she was asking everybody, have you seen my people?
And somebody-- people were wandering around all over Europe those days looking for families.
And somebody who had been in Italy came, and told her, yeah, your son is in Italy.
So she started making her way across Europe, and eventually, in Austria, she sent me a postcard.
And the postcard got to me somehow.
It was amazing.
And this is how I knew she was alive.
And then she got to me in Italy.
I guess it was sometime the middle of 1946.
And we met, you know, and she got a job, at first, in a British army canteen.
I used to come down from my camp whenever I could hitch a ride, and she would give me sardine sandwiches, which were delicious on British white bread and stuff.
And then she met this very nice man who had lost his whole family in the Warsaw ghetto, and they got married.
And he was already in Italy into the black market, so he had some money.
And they bought a visa to go to South America and went off to Venezuela.

Me, I stayed in Europe, because ORT, which is an organization that apparently had been founded way back under the czar by one of my uncles-- or a group of people including my uncle-- found out that I was alive.
And they said they'd give me a scholarship to go to school, to finish school in Switzerland or in France or in Italy.
And naturally, I picked Switzerland because I spoke German.
So I went to Switzerland.
My stepfather and my mother went off to South America.
And I was in Switzerland for about six months going to school.
And then some very distant relatives from England that I didn't know existed showed up and asked me if I wanted to go live in England-- to England with them.
So I went to England, and for about another six months I went to high school during the day.
And at night, I took correspondence course.
And then I was caught up.
I passed the equivalent certificate-- London University matriculation certificate examination, and went to South America.
Didn't like it at all.
And after about-- Where in South America?
Venezuela.
Caracas, which at that point was very primitive.
It had 400 telephones in the entire city.
Every business had to have a little boy who would run around with messages to other businesses if you wanted to communicate.
It was very strange.
It was a country that jumped directly from, from the horse era into the airplane era.
The only way you could get into the interior of the country was by plane.
There were no roads.
Very strange.
Anyway, so then I applied to New York University, and I went to it.
I was accepted.
I got a student visa.
And I went to NYU for four years and graduated, and came to California and went into business.
First I was a salesman.
Then I started a little bit on my own.
And then after about-- this was in 1953 that I came to California.
And I was married.
I married an American, and we had two sons.
In New York, soon as I graduated.
I graduated, and then I came to LA.
And then in 1969, I was in business.
By the way, in 1964, I decided that I was bored.
So I took-- I went to school, to UC Northridge at night, and I got a master's degree in history specializing in Eastern European history.
And gave me something to do.
And then in 1969, I went to a meeting of the Jewish Federation in Los Angeles here, which had to do with Soviet Jews.
And that point nobody knew zilch about Soviet Jews.
You know, you could walk up to anybody in the street, say Soviet Jews.
Oh, are there Jews in Russia?
I didn't know.
It's like Jews in Turkey today, or Argentina.
I don't know, you know?
There are some.
Maybe it's good.
Maybe it's bad.
So I went to this meeting, and there was a man, William Cory, who was telling about what is happening, and how it's bad and they don't let them out, and there's anti-Semitism.
And I was interested, you know, because I spoke Russian and everything.
And I offered my help.
I said, I'd like to come to more meetings.
I'd like to do what has to be done.

And the whole thing ended up, really, with nothing because the official position at that point was there should be two Soviet Jewry efforts every year, one for [INAUDIBLE], have a demonstration if you can, one for Passover, you have an empty seat at the table to remember.
That's it.
Don't do anything else.
Certainly don't demonstrate.
Don't write letters.
Don't make phone calls.
And I ran into this other crazy person, Zev Yaroslavsky, at that point, who was a starving student and hated all adults because they wouldn't take him seriously.
And I did, and I guess I had the same kind of a sort of mischievous set of mind as he did.
And we started doing stuff.
And eventually we broke off from the federation because they were not active enough, and there was all these rules.
You can't do this.
You can't do that.
And we started asking the people in Russia, you know, do you want us to make noise?
Do you want us to do this?
And what actually turned me on at this very first meeting is the message that we got from the Russian Jews, the few Russian Jews who were willing to actually speak out and all that.
And the message was very simple.
It was, why have you forgotten us?
Why have you forgotten us?
And that struck a chord because I knew they had forgotten us when we were right after the war sitting in the German camps.
When you came to the United States, did you talk about your experience?
No.
No.
I did not think it was relevant or interesting.
I went to NYU.
I lived on campus-- first in a dorm, then a fraternity house.
I did not have any contact with anybody who was not American.
I don't think I met a single person in the four years of college.
Maybe I did, but I don't remember.
I spoke only English.
I did not talk about it.
Yeah, I didn't hide it.
You know, yeah, I was in camps, you know, so big deal?
I'd go out with girls.
I'd tell them about it.
I thought it was an interesting topic of conversation.
After I got married, my wife certainly knew about it.
My kids certainly knew about it.
But it was not an overwhelming focus of my life.
It was a sort of take it or leave it.
After I got into the Soviet Jewry thing, you know, and I met so many people and ran so many demonstrations and made so many-- you know, became better known.
And to most people, they didn't have the faintest idea that I was a Holocaust survivor.
And I didn't think that it was relevant for me to bring it up.
I got involved with the Wiesenthal Center when the Soviet Jewry movement sort of died down when we had actually won.
I mean, this is one of the few things that you can actually point to and say, yeah, we won this one.
People are free.
And that's what I went-- I always liked Cooper-- Abe Cooper of the Wiesenthal Center.
He was always extremely helpful whenever we needed help with the Soviet Jewry thing, whatever it was.
And quickly, you know, because anywhere else, any other Jewish organization, you can die from old age before it goes through all the committees and commissions and all the decisions.
I would go to Abe and he would say yes or no.
So I have a great deal of respect.
And then somehow or other, almost by the back door, I got into the Holocaust thing and into talking, speaking at schools and, and doing all that.
And this is what I've been doing ever since, more and more and more, as there is less and less to do of the other stuff.
Do you have any children?
Yeah.
I got two sons.
They're both adults.
One of them is in Pittsburgh.
The other one is right here in Sherman Oaks.
What's their names?
When were they born?
My older one is Michael.
Michael is 40 years old.
He is married to Shirley, a very nice young lady who comes from South Carolina, of all places.
And I have a grandchild by the name of Nicholas who is 12.
He'll be bar mitzvah next year.
And I have an eight-year-old little girl named Rachel, and we get along very nicely.
And my younger son, Larry, he's 37, and he lives in Pittsburgh.
And he's not married.
He was married, as a matter of fact, to a concentration camp survivor about 20 years ago-- 15 years ago-- and the daughter of a survivor.
Her father had been in Auschwitz.
And it turned out to be a terrible, terrible thing.
And it sort of soured him on marriage for a long time and just about destroyed his life.
I might parenthetically say that once upon a long time ago, I had a conversation in my kitchen with Dennis Prager, who is a man I respect who has got the radio program, wrote a number of books.

In fact, his book about the eight-- nine questions people ask about Judaism, we translated into Russian, distributed thousands, tens of thousands of copies throughout the Soviet Union and also among the immigrants here.

So Dennis was telling me that when he was a younger man, his first job as a summer camp-- a Jewish summer camp-- counselor, the director of the camp assembled all of the counselors the night before the camp was opened.

And he said to them, I just want to warn you, those of you who have not been counselors before, there's two categories of kids that you're almost sure to have problems with-- children of rabbis and children of survivors.
And Dennis said he doesn't know why that is, and I don't know why that is.
But I know that my Larry, who was a child of a survivor and was married to a child of a survivor, that marriage did not work out.
Whether it's my fault or Hitler's fault, I don't know.
Yes.
The one in Pittsburgh I talk to on the telephone maybe once every couple of weeks.
He is much closer to his mother than to me, always has been.
Just worked out that way.
The one who is here, oh, we see each other, like, every week or so.
The relationship-- we like and respect one another.
I really can't say that we have the really warm, huggy, touchy feely relationship.
It's not my nature.
Maybe that, too, has somehow been destroyed within me.
And maybe somehow-- I've thought about this a lot, why it is that I have-- I make-- it's so much easier for me to relate to groups than it is to individuals.
I'm totally comfortable speaking before 100, 150, 200 people.
I'm not all that comfortable developing a one-to-one relationship.
I'm not a very warm person, and I'm aware of that.
And I think that conceivably, maybe there is something in the back of mind that is afraid that if I get close to somebody, they'll be taken away and killed.
And things have happened in my life [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where a a number of people died that I held very dear.
And you know, and each time, I guess it reinforces it.
It's a stupid way to live, and it's a stupid way to-- and I can't help it.
No.
My mother died 12 years ago in Los Angeles.
And her death was truly tragic, not so much because of the fact that she died, but because she developed Alzheimer's about eight years before she died.
And after about three or four years, this beautiful, intelligent woman didn't recognize anybody, couldn't talk, had to wear diapers, couldn't sign her name.
The weird thing is that from time to time, she could-- she would still get up and sit at the piano and play a tune, which-- automatically, I suppose.
But she couldn't talk.
And I think that this is probably the most terrible thing in the world, to see a human being becoming a vegetable.
And now, each time, of course, I forget a telephone number, or each time I can't remember a name or something, I think, oh my god, it's happening, you know?
And I can only wish and hope that when and if something like that should happen to me, that somebody will take pity on me and put me out of my misery.
You know-- I-- that's why I think that Dr.
Kevorkian ?] is doing a good thing.
What are those things that you were telling me that, that were hurting you so much that you don't want to be hurt more by individuals?
It's losing people.
It's losing people.
It's losing my first wife, who divorced me.
She left me.
I can't blame her.
You know, I brought it upon myself.
I had become a fanatic with the Soviet Jewry thing.
I worked all day.
My business could have been much better.
I could have been much wealthier.
But the phone would ring every hour of the day and night.
And the press was there and the TV was there.
And I would develop programs, and I would run things.
And eventually I went to Russia with a group of other activists.
And theoretically, I'm a Soviet citizen.
I'm from Lithuania.
They could've kept me.
And my wife felt that I had put the cause-- because I went against her wishes, and I felt I had to go.
And I met with a lot of dissidence and brought out film out of there, which was shown on national TV, stuff like that.
And she felt that I was putting this cause, you know, over my family, so she left me.
It was a shock to me because I'm a very loyal person.
Once I develop a loyalty to somebody or something, I'm there.
And I felt that as a terrible betrayal.
And then after that, I had a girlfriend who had come out of the Soviet Jewry movement, and we were very close.
She was younger than I.
She was a very nice person.
And she died.
She had a brain hemorrhage totally unexpectedly, just collapsed in the parking lot of the Pomona Fair.
Just like that, never regained consciousness.
So that was a shock.
And then I met another lady, and we eventually got married.
That was Kathy, my wife.
And we were together for something like 16 years.
And she was very, very sick over the last five, six years.
It was a miserable life for me, miserable life for her.
She was almost always in pain.
And she died three weeks ago, a month ago now, almost a month.
So there we are.
So don't come too close to me.
Bad things will happen.
Kidding.
Yeah.
Kathy has a daughter, Michelle, whom I brought up from the time she was five years old, and we are quite close.
Very nice, very pretty, very bright young lady who turned out very well in spite of being brought up by a survivor.
So who knows?
Maybe there's nothing to it.
She was five.
That's your child?
No.
That's not your child.
No, it's not my child, no.
Her dad is here in California.
You know, nice man.
I've got nothing against him.
But no, I was really, for all practical purposes, was and am her Dad.
And so there is that.
Yes.
Well, to begin with, I just want to express some words of gratitude, really.
I had a hell of a good life, all things considered.
I can only think that I must have been spared for a reason.
And even if I wasn't spared for a reason, I should believe that I was and somehow repay that.
And I try to do what I can to repay it.
Whether I'm successful or not, I can't judge, but I'm doing my best.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] words of advice?
What can I say?
It's-- I know so many people that have been saved by Gentiles.
I know that I was saved by Gentiles, the people who liked my father and respected my father.
I think that it is important not to paint everybody with the same brush just because they are a certain nationality or a certain background or whatever it is.

At the same time, I think it's very stupid to assume that everybody's a good person, that there is really no good and evil, and that maybe whoever is an evil person is-- well, maybe they're just sick, or maybe they had a bad childhood or a bad experience.
I don't believe that at all.
I think that there is evil and there is good.
I think that evil people never think they're evil.
I don't think Hitler ever got up in the morning and said, today I'm really going to do something nasty.
I'm sure he was totally convinced that he was doing wonderful stuff cleaning up the world.
So just because the intentions are good, I think-- I think that doesn't mean anything, whatever you intend to do.
I think what is important is what you actually do, and what the results are of what you do.
And I think that it is important for anybody who sees this film, if they ever-- if anybody ever does, to realize that everything we do influences the future, that the future is completely liquid.
That if, for example, Hitler had not invaded Russia, today we'd probably be living in a world that would be fascist.
And there would be no Jews or no blacks and no Indians left alive anywhere in the world.
And well-meaning people would probably go to a zoo on a Sunday and look in a glass cage to see a real Jew or a real black because all the rest of them would be dead.
And anything you do today will influence the future.
And all I can say is we should continue trying to do whatever it is that will influence the future for the better, for the better for all, not just the better for the individual.
It is important insofar as that much of my life has revolved around causes and around things connected with Judaism.
If I were asked to define why it is that I feel myself to be a Jew, I would find it extremely difficult.
The best I could come up with, really, would be I'm a Jew because I know that I am a Jew and because other people assume that I am a Jew.
I am not religious.
I find it very difficult to believe in a personal god, a god who is concerned with an individual, specifically.
I find it difficult to observe the rituals because I believe that if God is indeed all knowing, that he knows what is in everybody's heart.
And there is no need for me to stay there-- stand there and tell God how wonderful I think he is and how much I love him because he knows whether I mean it or not.
This is a personal thing, and I, I can go through the paces.
But I don't really believe.

At the same time, I'm extremely envious of those who do because it is so much more comforting and so much better for a life to be so absolutely sure that there is an afterlife and that there is a power, and that you are fulfilling his commandments when you do the things that you're supposed to be doing.
It's comforting, and I wish I could.
But I can't.
A number of things happened to me personally over the last couple of months which lead me, or might lead me, to believe, you know, that there is a god, and that there is a god that takes an interest.
No, I'd rather not.
But but even the fact that I managed to survive might indicate to someone of a different mental bent that I was saved in order to do what I have done, that there was a divine purpose in it.
I think it would be extremely conceited of me to believe that, that I'm somehow been designated an instrument of divine will.
I really cannot believe that.
I wish I could.
What I see in the future is, you might consider it an optimistic or you might consider the pessimist view, and it's the same view.
I think that if things continue to go as well for the Jews here in America, then within 100 years or so, there will not be any Jews in America.
We'll assimilate just like we have in Spain and in Italy and in Germany until something happened, and they started mistreating us, and this reminded us.
And a lot of people died in the process.

Now to say that the Jews cannot disappear--that there is a divine will saying that the Jews have survived for 3,000 years and therefore, they must survive for another 3,000 year-- is probably accurate, but it's not really historically true because there are 10 tribes that disappeared.
Where the hell are they?
Only two tribes were left that we know of and maintain the Jewish tradition.

And also, the Jews who are alive today really, technically, racially are not really Jews, if you look at all the blonde-haired, blue-eyed Jews in Eastern Europe, and all the black Jews in Ethiopia, and all the Chinese-looking Jews in China, there was an awful lot of mixed blood going around.
It was an awful lot of marriages and then fooling around going around.
Otherwise, we would all look like the Arabs.
So I don't think it's in the race.
I don't think it's in the blood.
I think that, to a great extent, it is that the anti-Semites force us to remain Jewish.
And I think that if the Jews do disappear through assimilation-- and we already have a 52% intermarriage rate here.
We already had an over 50% intermarriage rate in the Soviet Union, two of the largest Jewish communities in the world.
If it is permitted, if it is accepted, if it goes on as it is now, we might disappear.
Whether it is a good thing or a bad thing, I cannot say.
I do not know.

Whether would I have wanted that the Jews of Europe had assimilated 150 years ago completely so there would have been no six million Jews for Hitler to kill, but there would be today six million more human beings not Jewish running around Europe who would otherwise be dead.
I don't even know whether that would be a good thing or a bad thing.
It's not for me to say.
It's for somebody wiser than I.
But some people might say that this would be a good thing.
And others, of course, would say not.
Thank you.
